did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (a subset of AI) to classify skin lesions. Specifically, it analyzes 13 convolutional neural network architectures (DenseNet, ResNet, MobileNet, and EfficientNet) for binary and multiclass classification of skin lesions from clinical images. It also employs SHAP and CAM algorithms for interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the comprehensive analysis of 13 deep learning models (DenseNet, ResNet, MobileNet, EfficientNet) for binary and multiclass classification of skin lesions using the PADUFES-20 dataset. The paper reports specific quantitative results (e.g., accuracy metrics like 85.80% and 75.07%) and employs SHAP and CAM algorithms for interpretability, which are characteristics of an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Interpretable Deep Learning for Classifying Skin Lesions
brief_summary: This study developed an interpretable deep learning framework to classify skin lesions using clinical images from the PADUFES-20 dataset, which contains 2,298 samples of six lesion types. The researchers analyzed 13 convolutional neural network architectures from DenseNet, ResNet, MobileNet, and EfficientNet families, evaluating their performance in both binary and multiclass classification tasks. The models were trained with various data augmentation strategies and optimization algorithms, including AdamW, Adam, and Rectified Adam. In binary classification, the EfficientNet-B7 model initialized with ImageNet pretrained weights achieved the highest accuracy of 85.80%, while DenseNet-161 reached 79.40% with random weights. For multiclass classification, DenseNet121 with pretrained weights attained a top accuracy of 75.07%, whereas the best random-weight model achieved 65.1%. The study utilized SHAP and CAM algorithms to provide interpretability insights, highlighting the potential of smartphone-captured clinical images for accessible skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant lesion class included in the PADUFES-20 dataset used for binary and multiclass classification experiments.

Grouped class for binary classification task, comprising BCC, MEL, and SCC from the PADUFES-20 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary (benign vs. malignant) and Multiclass (six specific lesion types: BCC, MEL, SCC, ACK, NEV, SEK)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly investigates binary and multiclass classification of skin lesions from clinical images using 13 deep learning models. The primary task is classification, as evidenced by the reporting of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics for discrete diagnostic categories. While the paper mentions 'localizing' lesions via CAM algorithms for interpretability, this is secondary to the classification objective and does not constitute a primary detection/localization task in the context of the model's main prediction output.

datasets_used.name: PADUFES-20
datasets_used.description: The first publicly available skin lesion dataset sourced from smartphone cameras, containing clinical images of dermal lesions along with patient demographics. It was used as the primary data source for this study.

use.training: Used for training 13 deep learning models (DenseNet, ResNet, MobileNet, EfficientNet) with and without data augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation during model development and hyperparameter tuning.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluating model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC, BACC) on binary and multiclass classification tasks.
use.other: Used for interpretability analysis via SHAP and CAM algorithms.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the mean performance difference between original (without data augmentation) and data augmentation schemes for the same optimizer, and to assess statistical significance of performance improvements.

To compare the mean performances assuming unequal variances between original (without data augmentation) and data augmentation schemes, and to assess statistical significance of performance improvements.
To determine the significance of the observed differences in model performance between data augmentation schemes and instances without data augmentation.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the mean performance between the original dataset (without data augmentation) and the data augmentation schemes for the same optimizer.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the mean performance between the original dataset (without data augmentation) and the data augmentation schemes for the same optimizer.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The resulting p-values were used to determine the significance of the observed differences in model performance. Specific p-values reported include 0.95 (paired and Welch t-tests for DenseNet161 DA-1 vs original), 0.07 and 0.09 (paired and Welch t-tests for DenseNet161 DA-3 vs original), 0.015 and 0.0003 (paired and Welch t-tests for DenseNet121 DA-3 vs original), 0.01 and 0.02 (paired and Welch t-tests for ResNet-50 DA-5 vs original), 0.013 and 0.012 (paired and Welch t-tests for EfficientNet-b0 DA-4 vs original), and 0.02 and 0.0018 (paired and Welch t-tests for MobileNet-V3S DA-4 vs original). The paper notes that lower p-values (e.g., 0.015, 0.0003, 0.01, 0.02, 0.013, 0.012, 0.02, 0.0018) demonstrated 'high levels of statistical difference' or 'statistical significance' in performance improvements due to data augmentation.

binary_classification_random_weights.description: Models trained from scratch (random weights) on original images without data augmentation, evaluated on testing set.
binary_classification_random_weights.notes: DenseNet161 achieved the top accuracy of 79.40% with Adam optimizer when data augmentation was applied (specifically DA-3 scheme). DenseNet models generally outperformed other architectures.

binary_classification_pretrained_weights.description: Models initialized with ImageNet pretrained weights, evaluated on testing set. Best results obtained with DA-4/DA-5 strategies.
binary_classification_pretrained_weights.notes: EfficientNet-B7 with pretrained weights and DA-5 achieved the highest accuracy of 85.80%. DenseNet161 with random weights achieved 79.40% (from scratch). Statistical significance was observed for several models with data augmentation (e.g., DenseNet161 DA-3 vs original p=0.07/0.09; EfficientNet-B7 DA-5 vs original p=0.04/0.02).

multiclass_classification_random_weights.description: Models trained from scratch (random weights) on clinical lesion images for 6-class classification.
multiclass_classification_random_weights.notes: DenseNet121 with random weights and AdamW optimizer achieved the best accuracy of 65.1%. Statistical tests showed high significance for DenseNet121 DA-3 (p=0.015/0.0003), ResNet50 DA-5 (p=0.01/0.02), EfficientNet-b0 DA-4 (p=0.013/0.012), and MobileNet-V3S DA-4 (p=0.02/0.0018) compared to no augmentation.

multiclass_classification_pretrained_wei.description: Models initialized with ImageNet pretrained weights for 6-class classification.
multiclass_classification_pretrained_wei.notes: DenseNet121 with pretrained weights attained a top accuracy of 75.07% in multiclass classification.

architecture_used.main_model: 13 Convolutional Neural Network architectures from four families: DenseNet (DenseNet121, DenseNet161, DenseNet169, DenseNet201), ResNet (ResNet-18, ResNet-34, ResNet-50, ResNet-101), MobileNet (MobileNetV3-S, MobileNetV3-L), and EfficientNet (EfficientNet-B0, EfficientNet-B4, EfficientNet-B7).
architecture_used.pretraining: Two initialization strategies were used: (1) Random weights (training from scratch), and (2) Pretrained weights initialized from ImageNet.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in the provided text.

architecture_details.backbone_details: ResNet paradigm expressed as y = H(x, θk) + θs * x. DenseNet models utilized dense connectivity. MobileNetV3 variants included Small (S) and Large (L) versions. EfficientNet variants included B0, B4, and B7.

tasks: Multiclass classification (6 classes: BCC, MEL, SCC, ACK, NEV, SEK)

classification_or_output_head.binary_task: Binary output layer for benign vs. malignant classification.
classification_or_output_head.multiclass_task: 6-class output layer for specific lesion type classification.
classification_or_output_head.top_performing_binary_pretrained: EfficientNet-B7 with Adam/AdamW optimizer and DA-5 augmentation (85.80% accuracy).
classification_or_output_head.top_performing_binary_random: DenseNet161 with Adam optimizer and DA-3 augmentation (79.40% accuracy).
classification_or_output_head.top_performing_multiclass_pretrained: DenseNet121 with RAdam optimizer and DA-3 augmentation (75.07% accuracy).
classification_or_output_head.top_performing_multiclass_random: DenseNet121 with AdamW optimizer and DA-3 augmentation (65.10% accuracy).

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation (DA) strategies applied during training: DA-1 (Random resized crop to 224x224 pixels), DA-2 (Color jitter: adjusting contrast, saturation, brightness within [0,1] and hue within [0,0.5]), DA-3 (Random horizontal flip), DA-4 (Random rotation between -25 and +25 degrees), DA-5 (Combination of DA-3, DA-4, and DA-1 applied in order)
Duplicate removal strategy for the PADUFES-20 dataset (undersampling scenario) to retain unique image instances and create a balanced dataset
Image resizing to 224x224 pixels (as part of DA-1)
Random cropping (as part of DA-1 and DA-5)
Color jittering (brightness, contrast, saturation, hue adjustments)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Randomly crop the images and resize the cropped image to 224 × 224 pixels.
Adjusting the contrast, saturation, and brightness within the uniform distribution range [0, 1] and the hue adjusted in the range [0, 0.5].
Randomly rotate the images between -25 and +25 degrees.
Apply schemes DA-3 (random horizontal flip), DA-4 (random rotation), and DA-1 (random resized crop) in that order.

conclusions: The study demonstrates that deep learning models can effectively classify skin lesions using clinical images captured by smartphone cameras, offering a more accessible and inclusive alternative to traditional dermoscopic imaging for real-world applications.

In binary classification (benign vs. malignant), the EfficientNet-B7 model initialized with ImageNet pretrained weights achieved the highest accuracy of 85.80%, while the DenseNet-161 model with random weights achieved a top accuracy of 79.40%.

In multiclass classification (six specific lesion types), the DenseNet121 model initialized with pretrained weights attained the highest accuracy of 75.07%, whereas the best-performing model with random weights (DenseNet121) achieved 65.1% accuracy.

Data augmentation strategies significantly improved model performance, with statistical tests (paired t-test and Welch’s t-test) confirming high levels of statistical significance for improvements in models such as DenseNet161, DenseNet121, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-b0, and MobileNet-V3S.

Interpretability analyses using SHAP and CAM algorithms provided insights into model decision-making, highlighting specific regions of input images that influenced predictions, thereby addressing the 'black-box' nature of deep learning models.

The use of smartphone-captured clinical images supports the deployment of AI-assisted diagnostic tools in diverse clinical settings where dermoscopic tools are not readily accessible, promoting early detection and treatment of skin cancers.
important_remarks: The study is limited by the small size of the PADUFES-20 dataset (2,298 samples) and significant class imbalance, particularly for malignant melanoma (MEL) which has only 52 samples and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) with 192 samples, leading to poor multiclass performance.

The dataset consists exclusively of smartphone-captured clinical images, which introduces variability in image quality and lighting conditions compared to standardized dermoscopic images, potentially affecting generalizability.

The paper lacks external validation on independent datasets; results are derived solely from the PADUFES-20 dataset, limiting the assessment of model robustness across different populations or imaging devices.

Multiclass classification performance was notably weak (top accuracy 75.07% with pretrained weights, 65.1% with random weights) compared to binary classification, attributed directly to the limited sample size for specific lesion classes.
The study does not report performance metrics for specific subgroups (e.g., by skin type or demographic), raising potential concerns about fairness and bias in diverse clinical settings.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by the use of a duplicate removal strategy to create a balanced dataset, but the small total sample size remains a critical constraint on statistical power and model reliability.
